title: Habit Stacking & Anchoring

content.statement: Piggyback new behaviors on established ones.
content.question: Which anchor habit in your day is underused for stacking?
sop: List your daily non-negotiables, things you already do every single day without fail (coffee, shower, commute, closing your laptop).
Rate each one's Anchor Frequency (how many times a day/week it happens, count) and Ease (how automatic and low-friction it is, 1-10) (see calculation, page 4).
Pick the anchor with the highest frequency and ease, and attach exactly one new small behavior right after it.
Keep the new behavior tiny until it runs without thinking, don't stack a second one too early.
Once automatic, use that same anchor as the base for the next habit in the chain.
questions: Which anchor habit in your day is underused for stacking?
What's one habit you've been trying to start fresh that would work better piggybacked onto something you already do without fail?
How many of your existing daily non-negotiables have zero habits stacked onto them yet?
content.equation_example: Anchor Frequency (how many times per week the anchor habit happens, count) times Ease (rate 1-10: how automatic and low-friction the anchor is) equals Stack Strength. Anchor Frequency 7 times Ease 9 equals 63. Higher means the new habit has a strong, reliable base to attach to; lower means the anchor itself is too inconsistent to build on.
content.closing_statement: Attach new habits to what already runs on autopilot.

caption:
Principle Nº 205: Habit Stacking & Anchoring

Piggyback new behaviors on established ones.

Ask yourself: Which anchor habit in your day is underused for stacking?
